1: "How long, LORD? Will you forget me forever? How long will you hide your face from me?" — the "how long" question is the lament genre's most honest expression of faith under desolation. Christ entered this question at its deepest point: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matthew 27:46, citing Psalms 22:1). The "how long?" of human suffering and divine hiddenness is real — Christ did not deny it or resolve it cheaply. He descended into it, making it his own, so that no one who prays this prayer prays it alone.
2: "How long must I wrestle with my thoughts and day after day have sorrow in my heart? How long will my enemy triumph over me?" — the interior suffering that accompanied Christ in Gethsemane: "My soul is overwhelmed with sorrow to the point of death" (Matthew 26:38; Mark 14:34). The sorrow in the heart, the inner wrestling, the apparent triumph of the enemy — these were Christ's experience in the Garden and at Golgotha. He prayed "may this cup be taken from me" — the authentic prayer of one who knew the full weight of what was coming.
3: "Look on me and answer, LORD my God. Give light to my eyes, or I will sleep in death" — the prayer for life answered by resurrection. "Give light to my eyes" — the dimming of the eyes was the physical sign of approaching death; the giving of light is the reversal of death. Christ slept in death for three days (Matthew 12:40: "three days and three nights in the heart of the earth") and woke to resurrection light. For believers united to him, the sleep of death is not final: "Our friend Lazarus has fallen asleep; but I am going there to wake him up" (John 11:11).
4: "...or my enemy will say, 'I have overcome him,' and my foes will rejoice when I fall" — what Satan and the powers declared at the cross. They believed they had overcome him: "He saved others; let him save himself if he is God's Messiah, the Chosen One" (Luke 23:35). But 1 Corinthians 15:54-57: "Death is swallowed up in victory... thanks be to God! He gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ." The enemy's premature victory cry was silenced on the third day.
5: "But I trust in your unfailing love; my heart rejoices in your salvation" — the pivot of trust that characterized Christ's entire relationship with the Father. "Father, into your hands I commit my spirit" (Luke 23:46) — the last words of Jesus from the cross, citing Psalms 31:5. Even at the point of death, the trust in the Father's unfailing love did not fail. Hebrews 12:2: "For the joy set before him he endured the cross, scorning its shame" — the rejoicing in salvation that sustained Christ through death.
6: "I will sing the LORD's praise, for he has been good to me" — the vow of praise after rescue. Hebrews 2:12 cites Psalms 22:22 ("I will declare your name to my brothers; in the assembly I will sing your praises") as the risen Christ's proclamation in the church. The resurrection transforms the lament of Psalms 13:1-4 into the praise of Psalms 13:5-6. Christ leads the assembly's praise (Ephesians 5:19-20; Hebrews 2:11-12) — the firstborn who has been brought through death to eternal life now sings at the head of the congregation of the redeemed.
